title: Android Internals & Process Model
description: Zygote, process death, SavedStateHandle, memory management, threading models, and app startup

references.title: Android Developers — Processes and App Lifecycle

lesson:
How Android Launches Your App

Understanding Android internals separates senior engineers from mid-level. These concepts affect architecture decisions around state, threading, and memory.

Zygote Process

Every Android app is a fork of the Zygote process. Zygote pre-loads the Android framework classes and resources so apps start faster via copy-on-write (COW) memory sharing.

System Boot │ ▼ init → Zygote (preloads framework classes, ART runtime) │ ├── fork() → System Server (ActivityManager, WindowManager, etc.) │ ├── fork() → Your App (PID 12345) ├── fork() → Another App (PID 12346) └── fork() → USAP Pool (Unspecialized App Processes, Android 10+)
USAP (Unspecialized App Process): Pre-forked processes waiting to become apps. Eliminates fork overhead on app launch — the process already exists, it just needs to be specialized. This significantly reduces cold start time.

Process Priorities & Death

Android ranks processes by importance using oom_adj scores. When memory is low, the LMK (Low Memory Killer) terminates lowest-priority processes first:

Priorityoom_adjExample
Foreground0Activity user is interacting with
Visible100Activity behind a dialog
Service500Playing music, downloading
Cached900+App in recents, no visible UI
Critical misconception: ViewModel does NOT survive process death. Only SavedStateHandle (backed by Bundle) survives. The Bundle has a ~1MB transaction limit — store only essential IDs and state, not large objects.

SavedStateHandle in ViewModels

class SearchViewModel( private val savedStateHandle: SavedStateHandle, private val repository: SearchRepository ) : ViewModel() { // Survives process death — backed by Bundle val query = savedStateHandle.getStateFlow("query", "") // Does NOT survive process death — only in ViewModel memory private val _results = MutableStateFlow<List<Result>>(emptyList()) val results: StateFlow<List<Result>> = _results fun onQueryChanged(newQuery: String) { savedStateHandle["query"] = newQuery // Persisted! viewModelScope.launch { _results.value = repository.search(newQuery) } } }

Application Startup Sequence

1. ContentProvider.onCreate() ← runs BEFORE Application! 2. Application.onCreate() 3. Activity.onCreate() 4. Activity.onStart() 5. Activity.onResume()
App Startup library: Many libraries abuse ContentProviders for auto-initialization (WorkManager, Firebase). The App Startup library consolidates them into a single ContentProvider with dependency ordering and lazy initialization.
// App Startup: define an initializer class WorkManagerInitializer : Initializer<WorkManager> { override fun create(context: Context): WorkManager { val config = Configuration.Builder().build() WorkManager.initialize(context, config) return WorkManager.getInstance(context) } override fun dependencies(): List<Class<out Initializer<*>>> = emptyList() }

Memory Management

  • ART GC — Generational, concurrent collector. Young gen (nursery) for short-lived objects, old gen for survivors.
  • Heap sizes — Vary by device (128MB–512MB typical). largeHeap=true in manifest requests more but is NOT guaranteed.
  • Bitmaps post-API 26 — Stored on native heap (not Java heap), so they don't count against the Java heap limit, but still consume device RAM.
// Respond to memory pressure class MyApp : Application() { override fun onTrimMemory(level: Int) { when { level >= TRIM_MEMORY_RUNNING_CRITICAL -> clearAllCaches() level >= TRIM_MEMORY_RUNNING_LOW -> clearImageCache() level >= TRIM_MEMORY_UI_HIDDEN -> releaseUiResources() } } }

Threading Model

  • Main thread — Runs the message loop (Looper). All UI updates, lifecycle callbacks. Dispatchers.Main
  • RenderThread — Offloads GPU commands from main thread (API 21+). Handles Canvas drawing, animations
  • Binder threads — Pool of ~16 threads for IPC (inter-process communication). System calls, ContentProvider queries
  • Dispatchers.Default — Thread pool sized to CPU cores. For CPU-intensive work
  • Dispatchers.IO — Shares Default's pool but allows up to 64 additional threads for blocking I/O

questions.title: Zygote Process
questions.description: Why does Android use Zygote to launch apps instead of starting each app from scratch?
options: Zygote provides security isolation between apps
Zygote pre-loads framework classes and the ART runtime, so forked processes share that memory via copy-on-write — drastically reducing startup time and memory usage
Zygote handles permission enforcement for each app
Zygote compiles each app's bytecode before launching
questions.explanation: Zygote pre-loads ~3000 Android framework classes and the ART runtime into memory. When a new app starts, it fork()s from Zygote. The child process inherits all pre-loaded classes via copy-on-write (COW) — only pages that are modified get their own copy in RAM. This saves both time (no re-loading) and memory (shared pages).
hints: Think about what every Android app has in common
fork() on Linux shares memory pages until they're modified

questions.title: SavedStateHandle Pattern
questions.description: What happens when the system kills the app's process while this ViewModel is active, and the user returns to the app?
options: All state is lost — title, content, and suggestions are all empty
title and content are restored from SavedStateHandle, but suggestions is empty (must be re-fetched)
Everything is restored because ViewModel survives process death
The app crashes because the ViewModel is destroyed
questions.explanation: savedStateHandle.getStateFlow() is backed by the saved instance state Bundle, which survives process death. So title and content are restored. But _suggestions is a plain MutableStateFlow in ViewModel memory — it's reset to emptyList(). The app needs to call loadSuggestions() again. ViewModel does NOT survive process death — only SavedStateHandle does.
hints: What's the difference between SavedStateHandle-backed state and regular MutableStateFlow?
ViewModel is destroyed on process death — a new one is created

questions.title: Startup Sequence
questions.description: In what order do these run when an Android app cold-starts?
options: Application.onCreate() → ContentProvider.onCreate() → Activity.onCreate()
Activity.onCreate() → Application.onCreate() → ContentProvider.onCreate()
ContentProvider.onCreate() → Application.onCreate() → Activity.onCreate()
Application.attachBaseContext() → Activity.onCreate() → ContentProvider.onCreate()
questions.explanation: The order is: Application.attachBaseContext() → ContentProvider.onCreate() → Application.onCreate() → Activity.onCreate(). ContentProviders run before Application.onCreate()! This is why many libraries (Firebase, WorkManager) used ContentProviders for auto-init — and why App Startup library was created to consolidate them.
hints: This order surprises most developers — ContentProviders run very early
This is why libraries abuse ContentProviders for initialization

questions.title: Process Death Bug
questions.description: Users report losing their draft text after switching to another app and back. The ViewModel survives rotation but the text is gone after a long background period. Fix it.

checks.message: Use SavedStateHandle to survive process death

questions.explanation: MutableStateFlow lives in ViewModel memory. ViewModel survives configuration changes (rotation) but NOT process death (system kills app when backgrounded). SavedStateHandle persists to the saved instance state Bundle, which survives both. Replace MutableStateFlow with savedStateHandle.getStateFlow() for state that must survive process death.
hints: The text survives rotation but not being backgrounded — what's different?
ViewModel survives config changes, but what survives process death?

questions.title: onTrimMemory Levels
options: When the system is about to kill your process
When your app's UI is no longer visible (user navigated away)
When the device is running critically low on memory
When a large bitmap is loaded
questions.explanation: TRIM_MEMORY_UI_HIDDEN is called when your app's UI is no longer visible — e.g., user pressed Home. This is the ideal time to release UI-specific resources (image caches, large views). Higher levels like TRIM_MEMORY_RUNNING_LOW and TRIM_MEMORY_RUNNING_CRITICAL indicate system-wide memory pressure and require more aggressive cleanup.
hints: UI_HIDDEN relates to your app's visibility state, not system memory pressure

questions.title: App Startup Library
questions.description: Fill in the blanks to define an App Startup initializer for a custom analytics library.

blanks.hint: The App Startup interface to implement
The method that creates and returns the initialized object
Declares which other initializers must run first

questions.explanation: App Startup uses Initializer interface. create() runs initialization and returns the result. dependencies() declares ordering — if your analytics depends on logging, return listOf(LoggingInitializer::class.java). This consolidates multiple ContentProvider inits into one, reducing cold start overhead.
hints: The interface has two methods: one to create, one to declare order
Return emptyList() if this initializer has no prerequisites

questions.title: Dispatchers.IO Internals
options: They are completely separate thread pools with no relationship
IO shares Default's thread pool but can expand to 64 additional threads for blocking operations
IO is a single background thread, Default is a pool
Default wraps IO with a size limit matching CPU core count
questions.explanation: Dispatchers.IO shares the same underlying thread pool as Dispatchers.Default, but allows up to 64 additional threads (or the number of cores, whichever is larger) for blocking I/O. When a coroutine on IO is blocked, it doesn't consume a Default thread — extra threads are created. When the IO work completes, the thread may be reused by Default. Use limitedParallelism() to create sub-dispatchers with controlled concurrency.
hints: They share threads to reduce context switching
IO allows more parallelism because blocking threads don't do CPU work

questions.title: Bundle Size Limit
questions.description: What problem does this code cause, and when does it manifest?
options: It works fine — SavedStateHandle can store any Parcelable list
It may crash with TransactionTooLargeException because the Bundle has a ~1MB limit and base64-encoded photos are large
It causes a memory leak because ArrayList holds references
It fails because SavedStateHandle doesn't support lists
questions.explanation: The saved instance state Bundle has a ~1MB total transaction limit (shared across the entire Activity). Storing full-resolution base64 image data will easily exceed this. On process death/restore, the system throws TransactionTooLargeException and the app crashes. Solution: Save only photo IDs in SavedStateHandle, and re-fetch the actual data on restore.
hints: The Bundle is serialized through Binder IPC which has a size limit
Think about the combined size of base64-encoded images

questions.title: Process Priority
questions.description: Your app is playing music in a foreground service. The system is under memory pressure. What happens?
options: The foreground service is killed immediately to free memory
The foreground service has elevated priority (like foreground activity) and will be among the last processes killed
The music stops but the process stays alive
Android never kills foreground services under any circumstances
questions.explanation: A foreground service (with notification) runs at elevated priority — similar to a visible process. The LMK will kill cached and background processes first. However, under extreme memory pressure, even foreground services can be killed. They are NOT immune, just much less likely to be terminated. The system will try to restart them afterward.
hints: Foreground services show a notification — this reflects their elevated status

questions.title: SavedStateHandle with Hilt
questions.description: Write a ViewModel with Hilt injection that uses SavedStateHandle to persist a search query across process death, and loads results from a repository.

checks.message: Get a reactive state from SavedStateHandle

questions.explanation: SavedStateHandle is automatically provided by Hilt for @HiltViewModel. getStateFlow("query", "") returns a StateFlow that persists across process death. The init block re-fetches results if a query was restored. This pattern preserves user input (query) while re-loading derived data (results).
hints: Hilt automatically provides SavedStateHandle to ViewModels
Think about what happens in init{} when the ViewModel is recreated after process death

questions.title: onTrimMemory Implementation
questions.description: Implement onTrimMemory() in an Application subclass that handles different memory pressure levels appropriately. Clear image caches at low memory, release all caches at critical, and release UI resources when the UI is hidden.
questions.explanation: Memory trim levels form a hierarchy: TRIM_MEMORY_UI_HIDDEN (20) means your UI is gone — release image caches. TRIM_MEMORY_RUNNING_LOW (10) means the system needs memory while your app is running — reduce cache sizes. TRIM_MEMORY_RUNNING_CRITICAL (15) means the system will kill processes soon — release everything non-essential. Responding properly helps avoid your process being killed.
hints: The levels form a hierarchy — use >= comparisons, not ==
UI_HIDDEN is the right time to release image caches since the user can't see them

questions.title: RenderThread vs Main Thread
questions.description: What work happens on the RenderThread versus the main thread in Android's hardware-accelerated rendering pipeline?
options: Main thread handles all rendering; RenderThread is only for animations
Main thread measures, lays out, and records drawing commands into DisplayLists; RenderThread replays those DisplayLists to the GPU via OpenGL/Vulkan — so the main thread is free for the next frame
RenderThread handles measure and layout; main thread handles draw
Main thread and RenderThread do identical work for redundancy
questions.explanation: With hardware acceleration (default since API 14), the main thread runs performTraversals(): measure, layout, and recording draw commands into DisplayList/RenderNode objects. The RenderThread (API 21+) then takes those recorded commands and replays them to the GPU. This means the main thread can start processing the next frame's input/animations while the RenderThread is still drawing the current frame. This is why invalidate() is cheap with HW accel — only the changed RenderNode is re-recorded, and the RenderThread handles actual GPU submission.
hints: Think about what 'recording' draw commands means vs 'executing' them
The split lets two frames overlap: one being recorded, one being drawn
